## [00:00 - 03:07]

Jay Abraham here, welcome back. This session is all important. This session may define your success probability and how the market sees you and your team, your product, your service, your brand, and your differentiation more than anything we've taught you. This session is all about trust building. It's all about how to create the connection with your market, with your team, with your vendors, with your community marketplace influences, where you are seen as the most trustworthy, the most authentic, the most unbreachably qualitative and pure representation of the best interests of the marketplace. Covey, who wrote the profoundly successful, stunning book, Seven Habits of Highly Effective People. Stephen M. R., the son, along with his partner, Greg Link, did breakthrough research on the impact and the implications that high trust organizations and leaders achieved over companies that have low or no trust. Stephen M. R. Covey and Greg Link did breakthrough research. They did outrageous discoveries that companies that enjoyed the highest level of trust amongst their marketplace buyers, prospects, their vendors, and their team produced results that on average tripled the results of their less trustworthy competitors. By that I mean if a competitor did a million dollars, the more high trust company would do three. If the competitor was making 15% profit, the high trust company might make 45%. If the competitor had employees leaving every six months, the high trust companies would have employees staying three years.

## [12:13 - 15:15]

I don't believe your culture is very strong on recognizing that everybody's life, everybody's place matters. Everyone matters. You matter just as much as I do. The housekeeper in the hotel matters. ## [21:25 - 24:31]

growing yourself, grow your understanding of your business. There are three factors you have to start developing and they all are elements of one another. Hindsight, which is the ability to look at what is happening in the future and what lessons can be learned. And then foresight, the ability to see what's happening in the world, what's happening in technology, what's happening with consumer attitudes, what's happening with direct and indirect competition so you can be the most knowledgeable and the most evolved at understanding and harnessing all this knowledge. The next one and a very important one is clarify expectations. What does that mean? It means that no two people always hear the same message when people talk. No two people have the same experiences in their life so they interpret phrases and ideas based on their unique experiences. No two people have the same value basis. No two people have the same definitions. So if you as a leader, as an entrepreneur, want to make sure your prospects, your clients, your team members, your vendors are all committed to the same noble purpose and the same lofty goal, you want to clarify expectations. You don't just want to say let's go sell the market because selling the market to one person might mean going and selling a bunch of little people a five dollar product into another person. It might mean selling a five hundred product to industrial companies. Whatever you communicate, you have a moral responsibility because you want to understand and examine and appreciate and acknowledge that every person you interact with, whether they're your team or your prospects or buyers, are having a different reality every minute of every day. So when you communicate anything, whether it's an offer, whether it's the attributes of your product, the benefits, whether it's a leadership directive or initiative to your team, you want to make sure that both sides said and heard the same

thing.
